Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (8) - BMI Calculator (1)

This one is basically a harvest of the last few chapters — a little practice exercise. I try to slip in a small assignment or two every few chapters, mostly because I want this series to be more than just reading about Python. Building something tiny is what makes things actually stick.

If you've ever learned a programming language, you've almost certainly bumped into the BMI calculator exercise at some point. So yeah, I'm going with BMI here too. The feature we're building is small, which makes it perfect for practice:

To meet all the requirements, we might end up using some syntax we haven't talked about at all yet. Assignments tend to be a bit harder on purpose, so this is also good Googling practice — you go dig up the extra knowledge you need on your own.
Enough chit-chat. Here's a BMI formula you've probably seen until you're sick of it:
Great. I'm sure you're already thrilled, because you know exactly how to do this now.
So that's it for this post, see you next time
Kidding, obviously. How could it end like that? So to start gently, let's write a nice friendly BMI calculator in JavaScript first — after all, we're learning Python from a JavaScript angle, right?
(I won't go into the logic and code here in detail.)
In earlier chapters we talked about how functions let you wrap up repetitive behavior. Notice that here, apart from
(which change based on user input), every other part is fixed. So we can wrap it into a function and just call it over and over:
Okay, now that we've done the friendly JavaScript version, let's get back to our actual target this time around: Python.

So how would we rewrite that BMI calculation in Python? Let's not crank up the difficulty right away — let's take it one step at a time and build a solid foundation before tackling anything harder. So first, let's try writing it the same way as the JavaScript version, before we wrap it into a function:
You can see the output has a ton of decimal places, but here we only want two.
So... how do we keep just two decimal places? Python has a function called
that helps us round to a specific number of decimal places:
the expression you want to round
the expression for how many decimal places to keep
One thing to watch out for: if you don't pass the
, it defaults to rounding off all the decimal places:
From the code above we can also tell that, again, nothing changes except
. So with a function we can write it like this:

def bmi (kg, cm):
  print('BMI result:', round((kg / ((cm ** 2) / 100)) * 100, 2))

bmi(68, 175) # BMI result: 22.2
bmi(75, 178) # BMI result: 23.67
bmi(45, 161) # BMI result: 17.36
On top of that, you can also try challenging yourself by rewriting the code with the
we learned earlier, to make the whole thing shorter and cleaner:

bmi = lambda kg, cm: print('BMI result:', round((kg / ((cm ** 2) / 100)) * 100, 2))
bmi(68, 175) # BMI result: 22.2
bmi(75, 178) # BMI result: 23.67
bmi(45, 161) # BMI result: 17.36
bmi = lambda kg, cm: print('BMI result:', round((kg / ((cm ** 2) / 100)) * 100, 2))
, don't you kind of want to rewrite all your code with

Of course, if you don't feel like using BMI as your practice exercise, you can pick something else — exchange rates, unit conversions, whatever. And if you're totally out of ideas, I personally recommend the calculators on
(in Mandarin). Pick any one of them and try building it for practice.

Let me also talk a bit about string concatenation here. Why bring this up? Mainly because everyone presents their assignment differently, and you might hit a couple of small gotchas along the way, so I figured I'd cover string composition while we're at it.
In the previous post we hit one gotcha about adding
together. Back in the early days of writing JavaScript, we mostly used the
operator to concatenate strings — this is also called string concatenation or string addition:
Then ES6 came along with a super handy new syntax called template literals, which is really convenient for composing strings — especially when you're building DOM:
What about Python? We've already tried plain string concatenation earlier, and if what you're passing in isn't the same type — say an integer (
) — then you have to do a type conversion:
This way of concatenating strings is honestly a pain — you keep having to type
symbols. So... does Python have something similar to JavaScript's template literals? Strictly speaking, yes — it's just that in Python it's called
Did you notice something? In that string formatting example, I didn't use
to do any type conversion, yet the example still runs without errors. That's because string formatting automatically converts whatever you pass in into a String type for you.
Doesn't string formatting feel way, way more convenient?
The main way to use it is to put a lowercase
at the very front of the string, then use
to insert the expression you want to compose — a variable, for example, works too:
works too — you just have to remember to use something like an IIFE pattern:
print(f'My name is { (lambda name: name)("Ray") }, nice to meet you')
So the function code from the previous post can be rewritten like this:

def fn(day):
  print(f'Day {day}')
  print('Wake up')
  print('Brush teeth')
  print('Exercise')
  print('Lunch')
  print('Nap')
  print('Afternoon exercise')
  print('Dinner')
  print('Evening shower')
  print('Sleep')
  print('---------')

fn(1)
Compared side by side, string formatting is cleaner and easier to write than string concatenation. And finally, here's a comparison of the same BMI calculation written in both JavaScript and Python:
And let's wrap up today's round with a meme:
Last but not least, let's do a quick little optimization of the BMI calculator. Continuing from the BMI exercise above, we can try adding some conditional checks to it, for example:
The arguments passed in can't be less than 0; if any argument is less than 0, the code should stop and output a message.
It should be able to tell whether someone is underweight, normal, or overweight, and output a message.
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum}, within the underweight range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum}, within the normal range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum}, within the overweight range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum}, within the mild obesity range.')
print(f'Your current BMI is: {bmiNum}, within the moderate obesity range.')
bmi(68, 175) # Your current BMI is: 22.2, within the overweight range.
bmi(95, 185) # Your current BMI is: 27.76, within the moderate obesity range.

Time to harvest what we've covered so far with a little exercise. I like to drop a small practice project every few chapters so this series isn't just reading about Python — building something tiny makes it stick. This time it's the classic BMI calculator, written first in JavaScript and then in Python, plus a look at Python's string formatting.
